text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic nascent fundoplication for a large sliding hiatus hernia. Pneumoperitoneum is established to a pressure of fifteen millimeters mercury. An optical view entry is made twenty centimeters away from the xiphoid process in the supraumbilical region. Two secondary ports are placed on either side of the optical entry for working instruments, and an additional port is placed laterally in the left axillary line to retract the stomach downward. A self-made three millimeter Nathanson liver retractor is positioned in the epigastric region to lift the liver and expose the hiatus area. The patient is positioned in a steep head-up orientation, with the surgical team standing between the legs and the monitor positioned at the head side of the patient.

The surgeon uses Hunter Graspers to reposition the contents of the large hernia defect, aiming to remove the entire hernia sac. Through the avascular gastrohepatic membrane, known as Kushner's membrane, the surgeon employs a harmonic ACE device to locate the right crux. Due to the patient's prior cholecystectomy, the hepatic branch of the vagus nerve is sacrificed during this process.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the right and left crura, aiming to remove the complete hernia sac while preserving the investing layer of the peritoneum. Small, precise bites are taken to avoid injury to the esophagus during the dissection.

The surgeon reaches the top of the two crura and excises the complete hernia sac, placing it into an endo bag. Dissection is performed both anteriorly and posteriorly to the esophagus, extending to the mediastinal level to ensure adequate intra-abdominal length of the esophagus for a 360-degree wrap placement.

The surgeon ensures the complete sac is dissected off while maintaining hemostasis. Care is taken to avoid injuring the vagus nerve near the anterior part of the esophagus. As the dissection approaches the apex of the left crux, the short gastric vessels are mobilized.

The surgeon uses the harmonic ACE to mobilize the short gastric vessels, freeing the esophagus and stomach attachments. The mobilization reaches the apex of the two crura, ensuring an adequate length of the esophagus. A retroesophageal window is created to facilitate the fundic wrap.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the esophagus both intra-abdominally and intra-thoracically up to the mediastinum, ensuring not to injure the posterior branch of the vagus nerve. The posterior vagus nerve is visible close to the esophagus during the retroesophageal dissection.

The surgeon reaches the gastroesophageal junction and frees it circumferentially, creating a window for retroesophageal dissection. A bougie is used for gastric calibration, and the two crura are visible. An umbilical tape is placed around the esophagus, secured with surgeon and square knots, and used to retract the esophagus downward via a port in the left axillary line.

The surgeon carefully places a retroesophageal tape around the esophagus, ensuring it is not tightened excessively. Fibro fatty tissue causing adhesions to the esophagus is meticulously freed, achieving complete mobilization while avoiding injury to the anterior and posterior branches of the vagus nerve. The procedure is performed near the pre-aortic area, with caution taken to prevent bleeding due to proximity to the aorta and pre-aortic fascia.

The surgeon removes the complete sac and performs crurography, mobilizing the right and left crura. Using a non-absorbable polyester suture number two on a 45 mm needle taper point, the left and right crura are approximated with U stitches to prevent tearing of the crural fibers. Three sutures are placed for complete approximation.

We believe and strongly believe that there is a pill available for reflux, but there is no pill available for obstruction. When we are doing the krurography, we should not make the oesophagus strangulate or get obstructed because of the tightness of the crurography. So one should leave some space and we believe on posterior crurography rather than anterior crurography. In this situation, We also know that anteriorly one may be tempted to do the Edson repair but again our apprehension is that we may cause more of obstruction if we do both anterior and posterior crurography. So we do only the posterior crurography.

After applying sutures, the surgeon tests the tightness by passing a bougie through the esophagus to ensure adequate space. A silk stay suture is placed at the highest portion of the stomach fundus, posterior to the gastrolytic area, leaving it six inches in length.

The surgeon performs the wrap by using a silk suture as a guide, ensuring the fundus of the stomach is pulled onto the right side without twisting. The shoe shine maneuver is executed to confirm proper positioning. Serocerosal gastrogastric sutures are placed on the wrap, including the esophagus in the first suture to prevent postoperative slippage.

The surgeon carefully places polyester or 2-0 Ethibond sutures to avoid injury to the liver, inferior vena cava (IVC), aorta, esophagus, and stomach. The right and left portions of the stomach are gently pulled together, and three sutures are placed to create a wrap approximately three to four centimeters in length. The wrap is positioned retroesophageal and is intentionally kept floppy, avoiding tightness.

The surgeon performs gastro-gastric suturing, securing the right portion of the wrap to the right crus to prevent intrathoracic slippage post-operatively. Right cruropexy is completed, followed by left cruropexy to ensure the wrap remains stable and does not migrate upwards after surgery.

take all the ports out under vision, Nathansan port also under vision and we request our NSS friend to extubate the patient on the table itself and not in the post-op recovery room. Patient should not have any retching in the post-operative period. Again, in order to avoid any slipped raps intrathoracic. So lot of precautions have to be taken. We remove all the pores under vision. We get the x-ray chest done in the post-operative recovery room and next day we do the gastrographin. Once there is no leak, the wrap is not intrathoracic, then we allow the patient liquid diet for two to three days and after that patient is able to take soft to normal diet and discharge on day two or day three. Thank you very much. Thanks a lot.
